title: Green Nasal Discharge in Dogs: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog's green nose discharge an emergency? Learn what it means, why it happens, and when you need to see a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Green nasal discharge indicates an infection or inflammation that requires veterinary evaluation within 24 to 48 hours to prevent it from progressing into serious conditions like pneumonia.

What does it mean if my dog has green nasal discharge?

Finding green discharge from your dog's nose can be a source of concern for any pet parent. In the veterinary world, green or yellow mucus typically indicates an accumulation of white blood cells, which is a clear sign that your dog's immune system is actively fighting an infection or significant inflammation. This discharge, known as purulent exudate, is often associated with bacterial infections, fungal issues, or even a foreign object, like a grass seed, stuck in the nasal passage.

How urgent is it if my dog has green nasal discharge?

  • Green nasal discharge is a Medium Urgency situation that requires professional evaluation and will not resolve on its own.
  • Untreated nasal infections can progress into more serious conditions such as pneumonia.
  • You should aim to have your dog evaluated by a veterinarian within the next 24 to 48 hours.
  • Seek veterinary care sooner if your dog is lethargic or refusing to eat.

How can a photo of my dog's nose help with triage?

  • A clear photo allows veterinarians to assess the color, thickness, and whether the discharge is unilateral (one nostril) or bilateral (both nostrils).
  • Discharge from just one side often suggests a physical blockage or a tooth root abscess.
  • Discharge from both sides more commonly points toward a systemic respiratory infection.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

An acute unilateral nasal discharge, potentially accompanied by pawing at the face, may indicate a foreign body. Chronic nasal discharge, initially unilateral but becoming bilateral or changing from mucopurulent to serosanguineous or hemorrhagic, suggests neoplastic or mycotic disease. Diagnostic steps include a thorough history, physical examination, and advanced imaging, such as CT, which provides excellent visualization of the nasal cavity and paranasal sinuses. Rhinoscopy, nasal biopsy, and deep nasal tissue culture are essential for identifying specific etiologies like fungal rhinitis, neoplasia, or foreign bodies. While serologic evaluation for aspergillosis can be problematic, combining seropositivity with culture identification of Aspergillus is highly suggestive of infection.

Chapter: Respiratory, Infectious Diseases, Neoplasia, Fungal

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1489)

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human cold medicine for nasal discharge?
faqs.answer: No, you should never give your dog human medications without veterinary approval. Many over-the-counter human decongestants and cold medicines are toxic to dogs and can cause heart issues or seizures.

faqs.question: What are the common causes of green snot in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common causes include bacterial respiratory infections (like kennel cough), fungal infections, nasal mites, foreign bodies lodged in the nose, or even dental disease that has affected the nasal cavity.

faqs.question: When does nasal discharge become a high-priority emergency?
faqs.answer: If the green discharge is accompanied by difficulty breathing, excessive bleeding, extreme lethargy, or if your dog's gums appear blue or pale, seek emergency veterinary care immediately.
